title: P6-T5: Add diagram management test coverage across MCP and HTTP layers
description: Add broad test coverage for diagram management across both MCP and HTTP surfaces. This should validate the new registry workflows end-to-end: create, list, search, rename, archive, duplicate, delete, and recover as applicable. The purpose is to keep the management layer trustworthy once many capabilities share the same underlying registry.
acceptance_criteria: Automated coverage exercises the main diagram management behaviors across exposed interfaces and catches regressions in persistence, metadata handling, and lifecycle operations.

title: P2-T3: Migrate in-memory state into persistent diagram-scoped storage
description: Move runtime behavior from process-bound global state into persistence-backed diagram-scoped storage. This includes loading existing state into the new model and making normal operations write against the repository/database path instead of treating in-memory maps as authoritative. It is the core migration step from architecture to lived behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Normal canvas state can be read from and written to persistent diagram-scoped storage, and the application no longer relies on volatile global maps as the primary representation of saved work.

title: P4-T4: Add destructive operation safeguards and confirmation boundaries
description: Add explicit safeguards and confirmation boundaries around destructive operations. This complements automatic backup creation by making dangerous actions more intentional and easier to reason about, especially when Claude is operating quickly. The design should reduce accidental loss without reintroducing excessive friction everywhere.
acceptance_criteria: High-risk operations have appropriate confirmation or guard semantics, backup creation is enforced before destructive mutation where required, and destructive flows are clearly separated from routine edits.

title: P1-T5: Add session middleware and activeDiagramBySession map
description: Introduce the server-side middleware and activeDiagramBySession tracking needed to resolve requests against the correct current diagram. This is the thin routing layer that turns session-aware requests into diagram-aware behavior across HTTP and WebSocket handling. It is intentionally foundational because many later operations need to ask the same question: which diagram is this session acting on right now?
acceptance_criteria: Server request handling can derive a session identity, look up or assign the active diagram for that session, and expose that context to mutation and load/save flows.

title: P7-T4: Add performance and load regression coverage for many diagrams and sessions
description: Add performance and load regression coverage for scenarios with many diagrams, many sessions, and larger amounts of persisted state. The architecture is single-node friendly, but it still needs evidence that the chosen approach stays usable as the new registry and sync systems grow. This bead should catch obvious scaling regressions early.
acceptance_criteria: Automated or scripted regression coverage exercises heavier multi-diagram and multi-session scenarios and provides enough signal to spot unacceptable slowdowns or resource issues in the new architecture.

title: Phase 7: Security observability and migration polish
description: Harden the new architecture for real operation. This phase covers migration from the legacy global-canvas model, validation and schema hardening, observability for sync/session/persistence debugging, and final polish for release readiness. It depends on the core architectural shift from Phase 1, while many of its sub-beads can be informed by later feature work without being artificially blocked on the entire diagram-management phase chain.
acceptance_criteria: Legacy data can be migrated into the new model safely, persisted inputs are validated and bounded, sync/session/persistence behavior is observable through logs or debug surfaces, and final verification confirms the roadmap is coherent and release-ready.

title: Phase 5: Session lifecycle and conflict coordination
description: Define how multiple sessions coexist around the same diagram without defaulting to hard locks. This phase implements session lifecycle tracking, same-diagram presence warnings, last-write-wins semantics for normal element edits, and narrow operation locking only for destructive actions. It depends on persistence and diagram/session foundations, but it should be able to proceed in parallel with sync UX and safety work instead of waiting for them to finish fully.
acceptance_criteria: The backend tracks active vs stale sessions, opening the same diagram from multiple sessions generates a warning, ordinary concurrent edits follow the chosen element-level conflict policy, and destructive actions are serialized briefly at the diagram level.

title: Phase 1: Diagram-centric architecture foundation
description: Shift the entire server from a shared volatile canvas model to a diagram-centric architecture. This phase establishes the invariant that every important unit of state is scoped by diagram and every interaction is scoped by session. It is the architectural foundation for persistence, reopen flows, safe multi-session use, and all later UX fixes. The key outcome is that later work no longer patches around a global canvas; instead it builds on correct routing boundaries from the start.
acceptance_criteria: Every element/snapshot/file operation can be resolved against a diagram context. Every Claude-originated request has a session identity available to the backend. The backend can determine which diagram is active for a session and no longer assumes one global shared canvas.

title: P1-T7: Unit tests for session scoping and scoping functions
description: Add focused tests for the new Phase 1 routing and scoping rules. These tests should prove that session-aware routing chooses the right active diagram, that scoped broadcast helpers target the right audience, and that diagram-scoped mutation helpers do not regress back to a shared-canvas assumption. This test bead protects the architectural pivot before later phases add more complexity.
acceptance_criteria: Automated tests cover the main Phase 1 invariants around session scoping, active-diagram resolution, and diagram-targeted operations, and they fail if code reintroduces global-canvas behavior.

title: P4-T3: Implement restore UX over persisted snapshots and backups
description: Implement restore flows on top of persisted snapshots and automatic backups so recovery is actually usable, not merely stored. This task should define the user-facing restore path for saved checkpoints and auto-created safety copies. The important result is dependable recovery semantics, not just the existence of records in a table.
acceptance_criteria: Users or Claude can restore a diagram from persisted snapshots/backups through supported flows, and restore behavior preserves the correct diagram context while integrating with safety/history rules.

title: P3-T4: Replace sync button UX with passive status indicators
description: Replace the current sync button-centered UX with passive, always-visible status indicators that reflect connected/syncing/synced style states. The reason is to remove a user burden that should not exist once auto-sync and ACK are in place. The UI should communicate state clearly without asking the user to drive synchronization manually.
acceptance_criteria: The normal UI no longer depends on a manual sync button for correctness, and users can see the current sync state through passive indicators tied to the active diagram/session context.

title: P6-T1: Add create rename archive unarchive and delete diagram tools
description: Implement the core diagram lifecycle tools: create, rename, archive, unarchive, and delete. These operations make named diagrams a true first-class entity instead of a thin wrapper around canvas state. The work should preserve the safety model, especially for delete-like actions.
acceptance_criteria: Supported lifecycle tools exist and operate on persisted diagram records correctly, including name updates and archive state transitions, while delete paths respect backup/safety requirements.

title: P6-T4: Add thumbnail preview and recent diagrams support
description: Add thumbnail preview and recent-diagram support to improve discoverability when many diagrams exist. This is a UX convenience bead, but it matters because the new model depends on users being able to identify and reopen the right saved work quickly. Recent ordering should follow actual activity, not arbitrary naming.
acceptance_criteria: Diagram listings can expose recent ordering and optional preview metadata such as thumbnails strongly enough for users or Claude to disambiguate old work at a glance.

title: P7-T5: Final polish docs verification and release checklist
description: Perform final polish, verification, and release-checklist work after the main technical capabilities land. This bead exists to ensure the full roadmap hangs together coherently: docs/instructions match the shipped behavior, tests and graph assumptions still hold, and the final result satisfies the user-facing success criteria from the plan. Treat it as readiness proof, not filler.
acceptance_criteria: The implementation has been revalidated against the roadmap and success criteria, final documentation or operational notes reflect the actual behavior, and no major roadmap hole remains unverified at release time.

title: P3-T5: Add sync retry and reconnect flows without manual intervention
description: Add retry and reconnect behavior so temporary disconnects do not require manual intervention or leave sync permanently stale. This task makes the auto-sync model resilient enough for real use and reduces the chance that session interruptions become silent data-loss windows. It should work with the ACK/version model rather than around it.
acceptance_criteria: Temporary disconnects trigger reconnect and retry behavior automatically, pending updates are reconciled safely after reconnect, and normal users do not need manual recovery steps in common interruption scenarios.

title: P6-T2: Add list search tags and metadata support for diagrams
description: Add list/search/tag/metadata capabilities so diagrams remain manageable as their count grows. This task is about information architecture for the registry layer: Claude needs enough metadata and query support to reopen the right diagram intentionally after days or weeks. Search should prioritize practical usability over fancy indexing.
acceptance_criteria: Diagram listing supports recency-driven browsing, search can match meaningful name/tag queries, and diagram metadata is stored and returned in a form useful for management workflows.

title: P5-T1: Track session presence heartbeat and active diagram membership
description: Track session presence, heartbeats, and active diagram membership so the server can reason about who is currently editing what. This creates the lifecycle foundation needed for warnings, stale-session cleanup, reconnect logic, and debugging. Without this, conflict signals stay noisy and untrustworthy.
acceptance_criteria: The backend records session presence with heartbeat or equivalent timestamps, associates sessions to active diagrams, and can distinguish live sessions from stale ones for coordination logic.

title: P2-T6: Add persistence integration tests and crash-recovery tests
description: Add integration-level coverage for persistence behavior, especially restart survival and crash-recovery style scenarios. The aim is to validate the user promise behind this phase: diagrams can be saved, reopened, and recovered without trusting one long-lived process. These tests should target system behavior, not just individual SQL helpers.
acceptance_criteria: Automated tests demonstrate that persisted diagrams survive restart, diagram reopen flows work against stored data, and common crash/restart recovery paths do not strand or corrupt saved diagrams.

title: P3-T3: Scope websocket fanout and sync notifications by session and diagram
description: Refine sync fanout and status notifications so they follow the same session/diagram scoping rules as the new architecture. This task prevents unrelated sessions from seeing sync noise and keeps ACK-driven state meaningful. It complements the earlier broadcast-scoping work with sync-specific behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Sync-related websocket notifications are only delivered to relevant session/diagram viewers, and sync state transitions remain coherent when multiple sessions exist simultaneously.

title: P3-T1: Remove manual sync dependency and introduce auto-sync queue
description: Remove the manual-sync assumption by introducing an automatic sync queue or equivalent always-on mutation flow. This is the first step toward making backend state converge continuously instead of waiting for an explicit user click. The task should preserve loop-avoidance discipline while eliminating the current fragile UX dependency.
acceptance_criteria: User or Claude edits are queued/flushed automatically to the backend during normal operation, and the normal workflow no longer depends on an explicit manual sync action.

title: P4-T1: Add automatic pre-destructive backups for clear import restore and delete
description: Create automatic backups before destructive operations such as clear, import, restore, and delete. This is a safety-first task meant to reduce irreversible loss from mistakes or surprising tool behavior. The system should make backup creation a default protective action, not an optional extra the user must remember.
acceptance_criteria: Supported destructive operations automatically create a recoverable backup first, and backup creation is linked clearly enough to the affected diagram and session context for later restore.

title: Phase 2: Persistence and reopen flows
description: Add durable persistence and resume flows so diagrams survive restart and can be reopened days later. This phase introduces SQLite WAL as the source of truth, replaces process-bound recovery assumptions, and makes named diagrams discoverable through create/list/load/get-active flows. It delivers the first user-visible transition from ephemeral canvas behavior to a saved workspace model.
acceptance_criteria: SQLite initializes automatically, diagrams and elements persist across restart, create_diagram/list_diagrams/load_diagram/get_active_diagram work against persisted data, and a new session can resume an old diagram without relying on guessed process memory.

title: P5-T2: Add same-diagram presence warnings without hard locks
description: Surface same-diagram presence warnings when another live session is already active, without defaulting to hard blocking. This is the first coordination layer in the chosen three-layer conflict model and should improve awareness while preserving workflow continuity. The warning must become quieter once sessions are stale.
acceptance_criteria: Opening or switching into a diagram already used by another active session yields a meaningful warning, and stale/dead sessions do not continue to pollute the warning signal.

title: P1-T4: Implement scoped WebSocket broadcast by sessionId
description: Restrict WebSocket fanout so updates are only sent to the relevant viewing context instead of every connected client. This addresses one of the direct causes of sessions stepping on each other and keeps sync traffic aligned with the active diagram/session model. The important behavior is correctness of routing, not just reducing noise.
acceptance_criteria: Broadcast logic in server.ts can target the proper session and/or active diagram context, and clients that are not participating in that context no longer receive unrelated canvas updates.

title: P3-T6: Add sync e2e tests with reconnect duplicate and out-of-order event coverage
description: Add end-to-end coverage for the sync model, including reconnect paths, duplicate events, and out-of-order delivery concerns. This bead exists because sync bugs often look correct in unit tests but fail under lifecycle churn. The tests should exercise the full behavior promised by Phase 3.
acceptance_criteria: E2E tests cover normal auto-sync, reconnect recovery, duplicate event tolerance, and ordering-sensitive cases strongly enough to catch regressions in the always-on sync UX.

title: P7-T3: Add observability metrics and structured logs for sync persistence and conflicts
description: Add structured logs and observability surfaces for sync, persistence, session routing, and conflict coordination. This is critical because the new model introduces more moving parts, and supportability depends on being able to answer what diagram a session touched, what backup ran, and why sync drift happened. Prioritize actionable operational visibility.
acceptance_criteria: The system emits useful structured signals for diagram/session lifecycle events, sync/ack problems, conflict warnings, and destructive-operation backups, and debug surfaces are sufficient for basic triage.

title: P3-T2: Add sync ACK protocol between frontend and MCP backend
description: Add an acknowledgment protocol between frontend and backend so the server can tell whether a pushed update was observed by the active client. This enables reliable sync status, better reconnect logic, and more diagnosable event ordering. The goal is not distributed consensus; it is enough confirmation to support a robust single-node sync UX.
acceptance_criteria: Sync messages carry version or equivalent identity, the client acknowledges applied updates, and the backend can track last acknowledged state per session or diagram context.

title: P2-T2: Add repository layer for diagrams sessions elements snapshots and files
description: Add a repository/data-access layer that centralizes persistence operations for diagrams, sessions, elements, snapshots, and binary files. The goal is to avoid scattering raw SQL and persistence rules throughout server and MCP code, while keeping the mapping between plan concepts and code explicit. This layer becomes the durable API behind create/list/load/save/history flows.
acceptance_criteria: Reusable persistence helpers or repositories exist for the major domain objects, and higher-level code can load/update diagram-scoped data without duplicating SQL or bypassing schema invariants.

title: P1-T1: Add Diagram and ActiveSession types to types.ts
description: Add the core TypeScript types needed for the new model in src/types.ts, especially Diagram and ActiveSession-style structures. This task removes the type-level assumption that state is only a flat global element map and creates the vocabulary later phases depend on. Keep the types aligned with the plan so downstream code can enforce diagram and session boundaries consistently.
acceptance_criteria: types.ts contains diagram- and session-aware interfaces that can represent named diagrams, per-session active diagram tracking, and the metadata required by later persistence and coordination tasks.

title: P2-T5: Add snapshot persistence and restore path
description: Persist snapshots so named save points and backups are durable instead of process-bound. This bead closes the old gap where snapshots vanish on restart and prepares the restore UX used by safety features later in the roadmap. Snapshots should remain anchored to diagram_id, not to whichever process happened to create them.
acceptance_criteria: Snapshots are stored and retrieved through persistence keyed by diagram, survive restart, and can serve as inputs to later restore/backup flows.

title: P5-T3: Implement element-level last-write-wins conflict semantics with versioning
description: Implement element-level last-write-wins semantics with versioning and session attribution for ordinary concurrent edits. This is the default conflict policy for the first full version because it balances usability with simplicity better than hard locking or CRDT complexity. History and backups remain the safety net when two sessions touch the same element.
acceptance_criteria: Elements carry sufficient version/update metadata, ordinary concurrent writes resolve according to the chosen last-write-wins rule, and conflict-related history remains available for inspection or recovery.

title: P7-T1: Add migration path from legacy global canvas state
description: Add a migration path from the legacy global-canvas model into the new diagram-centric architecture. The plan assumes old elements, snapshots, and files may need to land in a default diagram without forcing manual recovery steps. This bead ensures existing users are not stranded by the architectural shift.
acceptance_criteria: Legacy global canvas data can be mapped into the new persistence model safely, typically through a default diagram path, and migration failure modes do not destroy access to preexisting work.

title: P4-T5: Add safety and history regression tests
description: Add regression coverage for safety and history features so the system keeps its recovery promises as later changes land. These tests should focus on backup creation, restore correctness, undo/redo behavior, and edge cases around destructive operations. This bead exists because the cost of regressions here is silent data loss.
acceptance_criteria: Automated tests verify backup-before-destroy behavior, persisted snapshot restore, and core undo/redo/history flows well enough to catch regressions that would weaken data safety.

title: P1-T2: Add sessionId routing to MCP server (index.ts)
description: Propagate session identity through the MCP surface in src/index.ts so every meaningful operation reaches the backend with a stable session context. This is required because session is the interaction boundary for active-diagram routing, sync status, warnings, and reconnect behavior. The aim is not a user auth model; it is operational scoping for Claude sessions.
acceptance_criteria: MCP tool handlers consistently attach or forward sessionId, and downstream server calls can distinguish requests from different Claude sessions without relying on one global process context.

title: Phase 4: Safety backups and undo/redo history
description: Add safety rails and recoverability for destructive operations and editing mistakes. This phase introduces persisted snapshots, automatic backups before destructive actions, and mutation history that supports undo/redo and post-incident recovery. It depends on persistence foundations from Phase 2, but it does not need to wait for the sync UX work in Phase 3 because backup/history semantics can be built in parallel once diagram-scoped persistence exists.
acceptance_criteria: Destructive operations automatically create backups, snapshots survive restart, undo/redo is available at the MCP layer for the active diagram, and recent mutation history can be inspected or used for recovery.

title: P6-T3: Add duplicate export import and restore flows at diagram level
description: Add duplicate/export/import/restore flows at the diagram level so whole diagrams can be copied, moved, or recovered intentionally. This bead extends management beyond CRUD into operational workflows users actually need when iterating or branching designs. It should work cleanly with persistence and the safety model.
acceptance_criteria: Whole-diagram duplicate/export/import/restore flows operate against named diagrams with correct element/file scope and do not bypass backup or restore expectations.

title: P4-T2: Persist element history for undo redo and recovery
description: Persist mutation history for each diagram so undo/redo and audit-style recovery are possible. This task stores enough information about creates, updates, deletes, and batch/destructive actions to reconstruct or reverse recent work when needed. It is the backbone for MCP-level undo/redo rather than a frontend-only convenience.
acceptance_criteria: Mutation history is written for relevant operations with diagram/session context, survives restart, and contains enough previous/current data to support later undo/redo behavior.

title: P7-T2: Add validation limits and schema hardening for persisted data
description: Harden persisted inputs and schema-related boundaries with validation and practical limits. Once data becomes durable and multi-session aware, malformed inputs and unbounded payloads become a bigger operational risk than in the old in-memory-only model. This task protects the new system against corruption and abuse more than feature misuse.
acceptance_criteria: Persisted payloads and key API inputs are validated against schema expectations or size limits, and invalid data is rejected or contained without corrupting the new diagram-centric store.

title: P5-T4: Add temporary operation locks for destructive actions only
description: Add short-lived operation locks only for destructive or structurally risky actions such as clear/import/restore/delete. The goal is to serialize the narrow class of operations that would be too dangerous under free-for-all concurrency while keeping ordinary edits non-blocking. These locks should be scoped by diagram and held only for the transaction window.
acceptance_criteria: Destructive actions acquire and release a diagram-scoped operation lock during execution, concurrent destructive operations are serialized, and ordinary editing does not become globally lock-based.

title: Phase 6: Diagram management completeness
description: Complete the named-diagram management model so many diagrams remain usable over time. This phase rounds out CRUD, search, tags, duplication, import/export, restore, thumbnail, and recent-diagram behavior so Claude can navigate old and new diagrams intentionally rather than relying on one active canvas. It should branch from persistence foundations rather than being unnecessarily serialized behind conflict work, because most registry management behaviors can evolve in parallel.
acceptance_criteria: Users and Claude can create, rename, archive, unarchive, delete, duplicate, search, and reopen diagrams through supported MCP/HTTP flows, and the system exposes enough metadata to manage a growing diagram inventory.

title: P1-T6: Wire all element/snapshot/file operations to use diagram_id and sessionId
description: Rewire existing element, snapshot, and file operations so they execute against diagram-scoped state instead of implicit global state. This task is where the architectural shift becomes real in day-to-day behavior: create/update/delete/import/restore-like paths must all stop assuming one canvas. The result should make later persistence and history work operate on the right diagram by construction.
acceptance_criteria: All core element/snapshot/file code paths resolve diagram_id and session context correctly, and no supported operation silently mutates a shared global canvas without diagram scoping.

title: P2-T4: Implement startup restore and session reopen flows
description: Implement startup restore, active-diagram recovery, and reopen behavior for sessions that need to continue work on existing diagrams. This task turns persistence into an actual UX by making saved diagrams discoverable and loadable after restart or in a fresh Claude session. It should keep the rules simple: list, load, restore context, continue working.
acceptance_criteria: After restart or in a new session, the server can restore persisted diagrams, load the requested diagram into active context, and preserve the correct session-to-diagram association for continued work.

title: P2-T1: Initialize SQLite database with WAL mode and full schema
description: Initialize the SQLite persistence layer using WAL mode and the full initial schema needed for diagrams, sessions, elements, history, snapshots, and files. This task establishes the durable data model from the plan and creates the storage contract every later persistence feature depends on. It should optimize for correctness and easy local operation rather than distributed deployment concerns.
acceptance_criteria: The database file is created automatically, WAL mode and essential pragmas are enabled, and the schema for diagrams/sessions/elements/history/snapshots/files is present and ready for repository-layer use.

title: P5-T5: Add conflict coordination tests for concurrent sessions
description: Add concurrency tests for the chosen coordination model, including same-diagram warnings, stale-session behavior, ordinary concurrent edits, and destructive-operation serialization. This bead validates the three-layer policy in practice rather than only in prose. The tests should prove the system is safer without becoming unusably blocked.
acceptance_criteria: Automated tests cover live-vs-stale session detection, same-diagram warning behavior, last-write-wins element updates, and operation-lock behavior for destructive actions.

title: P1-T3: Replace global Maps with session-scoped in-memory stores
description: Replace the current global in-memory state assumptions with runtime structures that can support multiple diagram contexts and active sessions. In-memory state remains useful as a cache, but it must stop acting as the only source of truth or as one shared canvas for every actor. This task prepares the runtime layer for persistence and scoped routing.
acceptance_criteria: The runtime no longer depends on one global element/snapshot/file map as the conceptual model; in-memory structures can represent state per diagram and cooperate with later persistence-backed loading.

title: Phase 3: Always-on sync and ACK UX
description: Remove the need for users to manually click Sync to Backend in normal operation. This phase makes sync always-on, adds acknowledgment tracking between frontend and backend, scopes notifications correctly, and replaces action-heavy sync UX with passive status feedback. The goal is to make synchronization boring, automatic, and diagnosable.
acceptance_criteria: Normal drawing and MCP mutations propagate without a manual sync button, the UI exposes clear sync state, reconnect and retry paths do not require user intervention, and the backend can track whether updates were acknowledged by the active client.
